coredata.dc:description: The design and fabrication of high sensitive and selective biosensing platforms areessential goals to precisely recognize biomaterials in biological assays. In particular, determination of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) as the main energy currency of the cells and one of the most important biomolecules in living organisms is a pressing need in advanced biological detection. Recently, aptamer-based biosensors are introduced as a new direct strategy in which the aptamers (Apts) directly bind to the different targets and detect them on the basis of conformational changes and physical interactions. They can also be conjugated to optical and electronic probes such as quantum dot (QD) nanomaterials and provide unique QD aptasensing platforms. Currently, these Apt-based biosensors with excellent recognition features have attracted extensive attention due to the high specificity, rapid response and facile construction. Therefore, in this review article, recent achievements and advances in aptasensing detection of ATP based on different detection methods and types of QDs are discussed. In this regard, the optical and electrochemical aptasensors have been categorized based on detection methods; fluorescence (FL), electrochemiluminescence (ECL) and photoelectrochemical (PEC) and they have been also divided to two main groups based on QDs; metal-based (M-based) and carbon-based (C-based) materials. Then, their advantages and limitations have been highlighted, compared and discussed in detail.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design and fabrication of high sensitive and selective biosensing platforms areessential goals to precisely recognize biomaterials in biological assays. In particular, determination of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) as the main energy currency of the cells and one of the most important biomolecules in living organisms is a pressing need in advanced biological detection. Recently, aptamer-based biosensors are introduced as a new direct strategy in which the aptamers (Apts) directly bind to the different targets and detect them on the basis of conformational changes and physical interactions. They can also be conjugated to optical and electronic probes such as quantum dot (QD) nanomaterials and provide unique QD aptasensing platforms. Currently, these Apt-based biosensors with excellent recognition features have attracted extensive attention due to the high specificity, rapid response and facile construction. Therefore, in this review article, recent achievements and advances in aptasensing detection of ATP based on different detection methods and types of QDs are discussed. In this regard, the optical and electrochemical aptasensors have been categorized based on detection methods; fluorescence (FL), electrochemiluminescence (ECL) and photoelectrochemical (PEC) and they have been also divided to two main groups based on QDs; metal-based (M-based) and carbon-based (C-based) materials. Then, their advantages and limitations have been highlighted, compared and discussed in detail.

ce:section.ce:para: ATP is an organic molecule that is the main energy currency of the cell and supplies energy to perform various processes in living cells. It consists of three main sections, a triphosphate, an adenine base and a 5-carbon ribose sugar. The triphosphate moiety is the reactive site of ATP and directly participates in different cellular processes and combination of two organic molecules of ribose sugar and adenine constructs the adenosine (AD) moiety of ATP (Fig. 1 ) [1]. Typically, ATP is produced by living organisms like plant and animal cells. In plant cells, it is formed from adenosine diphosphate (ADP) using sunlight energy during photosynthesis process and photophosphorylation reaction, while in animal cells, it is generally formed in the chloroplasts and mitochondria of living cells via a number of distinct cellular aerobic respirations and in the attendance of enzymes [2–4]. The extraordinary property of ATP is attributed to its reactive triphosphate site in which two phosphoanhydride bonds connect the three phosphate groups. The breakdown of phosphoanhydride bonds of ATP requires low activation energy and thus, ATP can be easily hydrolyzed and converted to ADP by simultaneously energy-releasing. Produced ADP can be also transferred back into ATP by cellular aerobic respiration and the ATP-ADP cycle is continuously regenerated with energy release [5]. Removing of one phosphate group generates 7.3 kilocalories per mole energy under standard condition (Fig. 1). This energy performs most of the processes that happen in the living organisms and this phenomenon has a fundamental role in the arrangement of cellular physiology and cellular metabolism [6,7]. Moreover, this necessary molecule is a precursor to build of DNA and RNA and macromolecules like proteins and it is utilized as a coenzyme for regulation of chemical reactions and acts as a ion transporter across different parts of living organisms [8]. Besides, ATP has a central role in signal transduction inside the cell and used for cell communication [9]. Taking into account of the different properties of ATP, wide attentions have been attracted for detection of this important target in so many areas, particularly in chemistry and biochemistry [10–12]. Recently, employing biosensors based on nanomaterials and quantum dots (QDs) are modern techniques for determination of this molecule in various samples [13–15].

Semiconductor QDs provide a new class of nanomaterials that reveal unique electronic, optical and magnetic properties [16,17]. These nanocrystal materials are in nanoscale dimensions (less than 10 nm) that are able to emit the light and are employed as fluorescent probes in biological determinations [18,19]. These fluorescent-emitting crystals have received huge interests as the fundamental materials in different fields of science, such as solar cells, laser technology, photocatalysis, light-emitting diodes, and particularly, biosensor applications [20–25]. The recent advances in the physic and chemistry of QDs and also, their biomedical applications have been discussed in a number of review articles [24,26–28]. The special chemical and photophysical properties of QDs are attributed to the following reasons; the size- and shape-controlled luminescence properties of QDs, stability of the QDs against photobleaching, the efficient luminescence quantum yields, high brightness, narrow emission bands and large Stokes shifts [29]. The existence of different reactive functional groups on the surface of QDs allows them to bind to a plenty of various organic molecules, peptides, nucleic acids, antibodies or aptamers (Apts) for broader and smarter applications [18].

Generally, the QDs that are used as a fluorescent probe in biosensors are two main types. The first type of QDs are formed from combination of the elements of the groups of IIIA and VA, IIB and VIA and IVA and VIA. Basically, among these QD semiconductor materials, the QDs consisted of the elements of IIB (Zn and Cd) and VIA (S, Se and Te) are only used as a fluorescent probe in biosensor technology such as cadmium sulfide (CdS), cadmium selenide (CdSe), cadmium telluride (CdTe), or zinc sulfide (ZnS) [30–33]. In this review article, the cadmium and zinc hybrid QDs have been considered as a M-based QDs. These QD nanomaterials offer excellent electrical, optical, and photophysical properties, including high brightness, tunable emission maxima, narrow emission spectra, broad excitation spectra, high thermal stability, long fluorescence lifetime, high stability against chemical degradation and photobleaching, and great fluorescent quantum yield. The second type of QDs that is recently exploited broadly in biosensing assays, especially aptasensing platforms is C-based QDs [34–36]. C-based QDs such as carbon dots (CDs), graphene QDs (GQDs), and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) have outstanding properties, such as low-cost synthesis, stable photoluminescence, good biocompatibility, great sensitivity, and high dispersibility that these features make them the great candidates for optical and electrochemical detection of various biological targets, particularly ATP [37,38].

Nucleic acid Apts are single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) or RNA sequences which are capable of binding to certain targets via electrostatic interactions, van der Waals forces or hydrogen bonding with high selectivity and high sensitivity [39]. They have shown excellent flexibility in the design and construction of a wide variety of aptasensing platforms such as fluorescence (FL), colorimetric, phosphorescence (PS), electrochemiluminescence (ECL) and photoelectrochemical (PEC), due to the conformational changes upon binding to their target molecules [40–42]. Besides, they have unique advantages, including long storage life, biocompatibility, low susceptibility to denaturation, versatility in labeling and ease of modification to facilitate their physical binding to sensing substrates [43,44]. They can be an excellent alternative for antibodies in biosensor technology due to their similar detection strategy and have more advantages over the antibodies such as high chemical stability under a wide range of pH, temperature, and buffer conditions, smaller size, low immunogenicity and adaptive modification which make them more suitable as detection elements in biosensors [45,46]. In addition, Apts can be used in approximately all immunosensors design with higher stability and better reusability compared with antibodies. Moreover, Apts exhibit a higher flexibility and more types of aptasensors can be developed thanks to their intra-and inter-molecular hybridization and exact sequence knowledge [47]. However, despite having many advantageous, the main limitations of Apts are their tediousness, high-cost, the length of time consumed using the SELEX technique [48,49], as well as their vulnerability to nucleases, particularly the RNA Apt which poses a threat to ex vivo and in vivo applications [49].

Recently, biosensors have been known as the user-friendly diagnostic devices for low-cost, facile, sensitive and selective detection of diverse target analytes [50–53]. In general, two types of biosensors have been developed based on direct and indirect detection of targets. Indirect biosensors act based on the detection of toxins and other biomolecules excreted by the pathogen under certain situations [54–56] while, direct biosensors operate based on direct specific binding of Apts or antibodies to the targets [57]. Apt-based biosensors are sensing platforms, including Apt as a bioreceptor probe which specifically binds to the target and then, the sensor converts a biological response into a measurable signal [58]. They are new and modern sensing platforms that recognize different targets with high sensitivity and selectivity. Thus, there is a pressing need to develop methods based on aptasensing platforms to detect various biological targets, specifically ATP. On the other hand, to improve the sensing, Apts are able to bind to the surface of QDs and the QDs aptasensing platforms are formed based on interaction between QDs, Apts and targets [59]. Up to now, some review articles have been published for the biosensing recognition of ATP. Jun et al. discussed a minireview based on the small-molecule fluorescent probes and two-photon microscopy for ATP detection in cell and tissue by categorizing their recognition elements, along with comments on their merits and limitations [60]. They divided the fluorescent ATP sensors to three categories of molecular interactions; (I) π–π stacking interactions with the nucleobases, (II) electrostatic interactions with the triphosphate moiety, and (III) binding with the sugar vicinal diols which can cause the selectivity toward ATP over ADP and AMP. Another review article about fluorescent probes for recognition of ATP has been introduced by Wu and coworkers [61]. They categorized the biosensors to five types of sensors according to the structures of the probes and molecular interactions including chemosensors using metal ion complexes, sensors that are able to form excimers, sensors with ability to interact with organic small molecules, sensors containing biomolecules and sensors bearing polythiophene. Moreover, recently a review article has been summarized by Li and colleagues based on Apt strategies for detection of adenine, adenosine, ATP and related compounds [62]. They focused on different and specific binding of RNA and DNA Apts with ATP and its derivatives and compared the limitation of the reported Apts. In these review articles, they did not discuss the recent and modern techniques based on QDs and nano materials.

The sensing mechanism of current ATP aptasensors is based on binding of ATP Apts to ATP molecule involves a series of intermolecular and intramolecular interactions among nucleotides and between nucleotides and ATP such as electrostatic interactions with the triphosphate moiety, aromatic π–π stacking with the nucleobase part or hydrogen bonding with sugar vicinal diols which leads to both structural and conformational changes [63]. For instance, as shown in Fig. 2 , a big structural and two-dimensional conformational change of an ATP Apt is observed upon interaction with a ATP molecule [64]. Thus, all of the ATP aptasensing platforms used this direct mechanism to convert a biological response to an electrical signal. Hence, in continuation of our reviews based on aptasensing platforms and QDs [65–69], in this review article, we mainly summarize and discuss most of the published achievements in aptasensing detection of ATP based on either M-based and C-based QDs or optical and electrochemical aptasensing platforms.

FL detection technique is a subset of optical methods along with PS, chemiluminescence, colorimetry and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) techniques [70]. Currently, optical methods, particularly FL technique have been developed due to their quick response, simple operation and high selectivity and sensitivity. Typically, FL-based aptasensors employ FL resonance energy transfer (FRET), FL quenching and FL recovery techniques for determination of different targets such as nucleic acids, proteins, peptides, toxins and other biomolecules [71–73]. On the other hand, as mentioned earlier, semiconductor QDs are fluorescent-emitting crystals that have been extremely used in biosensing platforms as FL probs [74]. Principally, both M-based and C-based QDs have been exploited in FL aptasensing detection of ATP which in following sections, different FL aptsensing platforms based on two abovementioned QD categories are discussed for sensitive and selective detection of ATP.

Among the M-based QDs, only Cd and Zn hybrid QD materials have been employed for aptasensing detection of ATP. In the pioneering research, Zhou et al. developed an aptasensing method based on CdTe QDs and chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) for ATP detection [75]. CRET is based on non-radioactive energy transfer between a chemiluminescent donor and a fluorophore acceptor which are in close proximity [76]. In this study, ATP Apt sequences were immobilized into the both QDs and horse radish peroxidase (HRP), respectively. HRP enzyme catalyzes the oxidation of different organic molecules like luminol in presence of hydrogen peroxide [77]. In presence of the ATP, it conjugated with Apts and brought the QDs and HRP in a close proximity and after luminol oxidation by HRP, the CRET was occurred specifically. CRET has some advantages compared to FRET, it does not require external light source and decreases the interference of autofluorescence and photobleaching. Interestingly, it was found that, other ATP analogues such as cytidine triphosphate (CTP), thymidine triphosphate (TTP) and guanosine triphosphate (GTP) did not induce the CRET similar to ATP and this method showed the excellent specificity for ATP. Also, this CRET aptasensing method could estimate the quantity of ATP by the FL intensity of QDs and the target could be detected with high sensitivity and selectivity in a low micromolar range (50 μM–231 μM) and the detection limit (LOD) was 185 nM.

A core-shell CdTe–CdS QD aptasensing platform has been reported by Tian and co-workers for another ATP detection based on FL polarization assay and antigen-antibody interactions [78]. Core-sell M-based QDs are the semiconductor hybrid materials with core-shell structure in which the binary QDs such as CdS, CdSe or CdTe QDs can be utilized as either core or shell and usually the shell is ZnS QDs, C-based QDs or SiO2 and TiO2 nanomaterials. With these structures, the optical and electronic properties of the QDs are extremely enhanced, which it is the main purpose of the biosensors design [27]. In this study, the core-shell QDs were bound to the complementary DNA and then, they were hybridized with ATP Apt modified with digoxin antigen. By adding the digoxin antibody into the system, due to the immunoreaction, mass-amplifying aptasensing platform was generated, which exhibited the high FL polarization value. When the target was added into the system, the FL polarization value decreased due to the release of antigen-antibody complexes form the QDs and produce the stable ATP-Apt hybrids. Regarding to the Perrin equation, when the target linked to the aptasensing platform, it decreased the binding ability of the platform to the macromolecules, so dramatically reduced the molecular mass and FL polarization value of the system [79]. This aptasensor detected the ATP in a low micromolar range (10–350 μM) and the LOD was 3.7 μM. Besides, this method showed the high sensitivity and selectivity and can open up new prospects of ATP detection in real serum samples and also it combined the sensitivity of immunoreactions with the specific recognition capability of Apts.

In the next study, the binary CdTe QD aptasensing platform has been introduced by Li et al. for ultra-sensitive detection of ATP based on FRET [80]. FRET is based on the non-radiative transfer of excess energy between an excited compound (donor) and an acceptor substance [81,82]. In this project, at first, the complementary DNA was conjugated on the surface of green QDs by covalent EDC, sulfo-NHS bonding and then, it was hybridized with ATP Apt modified-Cy3. Without the ATP, due to the conjugation of complementary DNA and ATP Apt, the QDs and Cy3 were brought in a close proximity and consequently the FRET efficiency increased. In the presence of the ATP, because of the higher affinity of Apt to its target, it was dissociated from the QDs and folded around the ATP and as a result, the FRET was interrupted. It is worth mentioning that, this aptasensing platform showed the high selectivity and specificity for detection of ATP compared to other ATP analogues like ADP, TTP, CTP and GTP which confirmed the successful design of the method. The observed LOD of this method was lower down to 0.01 nM with linear range between 0.1 nM and 1.0 μM.

Another CdTe FL aptasensing platform based on silver ions-assisted cation exchange reaction has been reported by Song et al. for sensitive and selective monitoring of AD [83]. The novelty of this study was the use of silver cation exchange reaction as a signal amplification of the probe. Cation exchange reaction is a versatile and simple approach to transform ionic nanocrystals into other nanocrystals with various shapes and compositions [84]. The exchange reaction can be occurred between silver ions and QD hybrid materials completely and reversibly at room temperature and quenched the FL intensity of QD materials obviously [85]. Hence, for construction the probe, firstly, two AD apts (ABA1 and ABA2) were respectively immobilized on the surface of Fe3O4 MNPs (Fe3O4-ABA1) and CdTe QDs (CdTe-ABA2). When the AD was added to the system, due to the high affinity of apts to their target, the stable sandwich structure was formed between Fe3O4-ABA1 MNPs, AD and CdTe-ABA2 QDs and showed the moderate FL signal. After magnetic separation, the FL signal of the probe was amplified with silver ions twice cation exchange reactions and this approach increased the sensitivity of the system for AD detection (Fig. 3 ). Besides, for evolution the specificity of the method, three analogues of AD such as guanosine, cytidine and uridine were investigated. The results revealed that only AD gave a significant FL signal due to the formation of the sandwich structure and confirmed the successful selectivity of the sensor for desired target. Moreover, the aptasensing platform exhibited the high specificity and great sensitivity for AD determination with a wide detection range from 0.333 nM to 167 nM and LOD of 0.217 nM. Furthermore, the proposed aptasnesor was successfully applied for AD detection in urine samples of lung cancer patients.

Srinivasan and coworkers developed a method based on both ternary CdSeTe QDs and CDs [86]. They developed the ATP detection using berberine and FRET between the Au or Ag NPs as a quencher and ternary QDs or CDs as a fluorophore. Berberine is an organic dye and it is able to form G-quadruplex structure in the presence of G-rich oligonucleotide sequences like Apt and emits FL signal [87]. In this study, in the first platform, ATP Apt was linked to Au NPs and QDs at 5′ and 3′ termini, respectively and in the next one, ATP Apt was linked to Ag NPs and CDs at 5′ and 3′ termini, respectively. In the presence of berberine, the system showed dual emissions at wavelengths of 650 nm from the QDs and 540 nm from the berberine, while, by adding the ATP, due to the formation of G-quadruplex structures, the FRET was happened from the QDs or CDs to Au or Ag NPs and thus, the FL intensity of QDs or CDs was quenched. The observed LODs for this labeled aptasensing platforms were reported 3.6 and 3.8 μM with a linear range of 0–50 μM.

Quaternary CuInZnS QD aptasensing platform has been reported by Chen et al. [88]. They used newly synthesized quaternary QDs along with Au NPs for AD determination. In this research, they immobilized AD Apt with a sequence of 5′-CC TGG GGG AGTATT GCG GAG GAA GG- 3′ on the surface of quaternary QDs and complementary DNA was linked to Au NPs. Similar to previous studies, without the target, two DNA sequences were hybridized to each other and the FL intensity of QDs was quenched by Au NPs. Conversely, when the target was added into the system, it bound to the Apt and restrained the hybridization between DNA sequences. Thus, the FL intensity of the QDs increased significantly. This aptasensing platform showed the excellent fluorescent responses toward target in the range of 50–400 μM with a LOD of 1.1 μM. This method was efficient for AD detection in spiked human serum samples where it exhibited the satisfactory results. The most important advantage of this method compared to previous studies was the using novel CuZnInS quaternary QDs as a new fluorophore.

Recently, an “off-on” phosphorescent aptasensing platform has been introduced by Xiong and colleagues based on Mn-doped ZnS QDs [89]. PS is a type of photoluminescence related to FL. Unlike the FL, phosphorescent materials do not immediately emit the light and the re-emitting process takes several hours after the original excitation [90,91]. To construct such a phosphorescen nanoprobe, firstly, they designed the 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) capped Mn-doped ZnS QDs and then, the ATP Apt was immobilized on the surface of QDs. Doping manganese on the surface of QD materials promotes PS emissions, and opens the way to develop the aptasensing platforms based PS phenomenon [92,93]. Briefly, when ATP Apt was added to the system, the PS intensity of the QDs was clearly quenched due to the aggregation induced effects and conversely, by adding the ATP to the system, because of the high affinity of ATP Apts to ATP, they dissociated from the surface of QDs and thus, the PS intensity of the system was gradually recovered (Fig. 4 ). This aptasensing platform showed a low LOD (0.9 nM) with a wide linear range from 2 nM to 9 μM. Furthermore, this PS aptasensing platform did not require complex pretreatments and could successfully remove the interference from autofluorescence and scattering light. What's more, the PS strategy was able to detect ATP in spiked fresh human serum and urine samples where it showed the satisfactory results.

Very recently, C-based QDs are used more for construction of biosensors and particularly aptasensors [94–96]. They have showed excellent features such as low toxicity, facile synthesis, super small particle sizes, biocompatibility and good stability in aqueous conditions and physiological environments [97]. These unique materials are able to be modified easily due to the different functional groups on their surface that allow them to bind to various biomolecules especially Apts physically or chemically [98,99]. As mentioned earlier, among the CDs, only CQDs and GQDs have been utilized for the construction of FL aptasensing platforms, thereby, in the following paragraphs, we discuss and review their features completely.

CDs have been discovered during preparation procedures of carbon nanotubes in 2004 [100]. These nanocrystal materials have nanoscale dimensions (below 10 nm) and with these sizes they exhibit FL properties. The FL from CDs may be attributed to the attendance of surface energy traps that become emissive upon stabilization as a result of surface passivation [101]. With these FL properties, CDs have been attracted scientist attentions in the optoelectronic, photocatalytic and biomedical applications [102]. In this regard and in a pioneering study, Xu and coworkers described a method based on terbium ion-coordinated CDs for fluorescent aptasensing detection of ATP with unmodified Au NPs and FRET [103]. Terbium is a lanthanide metal that is stable in air and has brilliantly FL properties in a bright lemon-yellow color [104]. It can amplify the FL emissions of CDs extremely [105]. Hence, in this research, authors used terbium to amplify the FL intensity of CDs and the system was built based on aggregation and dispersion of Au NPs. For construction the probe, initially, they prepared the CDs modified terbium ions (Tb-CDs) by microwave technique. In the presence of the target, ATP was linked to the Apts and the formed complex was able to coat the surface of Au NPs and finally, prevented the aggregation of Au NPs. Thus, the dispersed Au NPs could act as a quencher and quenched the FL intensity of the Tb-CDs on the basis of FRET (Fig. 5 ). It is worth to mention that the FL intensity of Tb-CDs decreased about 84 % after they mixed with dispersed Au NPs because of the quenching effect by Au NPs. Without the target and in the presence of Apts, Au NPs had affinity to aggregate. Therefore, they could not quench the FL intensity of Tb-CDs properly. The reported LOD of this aptasensor was 8.5 nM in the dynamic range of 40 nm to 4.0 μM.

Saberi et al. used a fluorescent aptasensor based on magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) and CDs [106]. In this research, 5′‐amino‐modified ATP Apt was immobilized on the surface of MNPs (Apt-MNPs) and a complementary DNA was labeled with CDs (DNA-CDs). Then, a double helical structure was formed with hybridization of the Apt-MNPs and DNA-CDs. Without the target, the FL intensity of CDs decreased due to the generation of double helical structure. While, by introduction the target to this hybridization system, because of the high affinity of Apt to its target, it was dissociated from the double helical structure and in consequence, the FL intensity of CDs was recovered. One of the most advantages of this method was using the MNPs in separation processes. Furthermore, molecular dynamic simulation studies have been perfumed in order to evaluate the interactions between the Apt and its target. Their studies showed that there are four hydrogen bonds and three hydrophobic contact points between the Apt and ATP that confirmed the successful formation of the complex. This CD magnetic aptasensor was able to detect the ATP in a dynamic range of 3.0 pM–5.0 nM with LOD of 1.0 pM.

A highly sensitive and selective aptasensing platform has been reported by Wang and colleagues based on FRET from CDs to nano-graphite for AD detection [107]. Similar to abovementioned articles, in this study, the CDs was considered as an energy donor and nano-graphite was employed as an energy acceptor. On this basis, at first, the AD Apt was linked on the surface of CDs and then, the nano-graphite was added to the system. Due to the sp2 electronic hybrid structure of nano-graphite, it was able to bind to Apt by π–π interactions. Thus, the FL intensity of CDs was reduced dramatically on the basis of FRET from CDs to nano-graphite. In contrast, upon target introduction to the system, due to the interaction of Apt and its target, the nano-graphite was dissociated from the system, and consequently, the FRET was interrupted and the FL intensity of CDs was restored. One of the most important advantages of this method compared to previous ones was the low toxicity or non-toxicity of used materials which could avoid environmental pollution. Besides, both CDs and nano-graphite are easy available and environmentally friendly materials. Under the optimized conditions, the LOD of this aptasensor reported 0.63 nM with a linear range between 2.0 and 50 nM. The similar aptasensing platform was developed by Cheng and coworkers. They developed the similar strategy based on FRET between CDs and GO [108]. They employed GO instead of nano-graphite. The LOD of this assay was 80 pM with a linear range between 0.10 and 5.0 nM. They claimed that the advantage of this method compared to previous one is the cheaper, more environmentally friendly and more available materials which we believe, this claim can be challenged because nano-graphite is more available and cheaper compared to GO.

Very recently, Saberi et al. developed another aptasensing platform based on CDs and cobaltoxyhydroxide (CoOOH) for sensitive detection of ATP [109]. CoOOH nanosheets are a type of 2D nanostructure that have FL quenching activity and because of the possessing planar structure and large surface area, they are a great candidate to be used in biosensing applications [110]. So, they used the CoOOH nanosheets to quench the FL intensity of CDs. Similar to the aforementioned strategy, when the Apt labeled with CDs was mixed with CoOOH nanosheets, they attached on the surface of nanosheets via non-covalent interactions and its FL intensity was quenched significantly. In the presence of ATP, the Apt labeled with CDs folded around of ATP with a strong and specific affinity. Thereby, Apt-labeled CDs were dissociated from the surface of CoOOH nanosheets and thus, the FL intensity of CDs was recovered. The LOD of this method was reported 4.0 nM.

Nitrogen and sulfur doped CQDs (N,S-CQDs) along with Ag NPs have been introduced by Yousefi and colleagues for the FL aptasensing detection of AD [111]. In this study, Yousefi et al. employed the N, S-CQDs as a FL probe and Ag NPs as a quencher. Concretely, doping heteroatoms on the surface of CQDs can successfully promote the applications and properties of C-based nanomaterials and encourages the electron transfer rate [112]. In this project, dl
-methionine was utilized as the N and S sources for doping the CQDs. This aptasensing platform was built based on aggregation and dispersion of Ag NPs. Thus, in the presence of AD, the complex of AD-Apt was formed and it prevented the aggregation of Ag NPs in a high salt solution. As a result, the dispersed Ag NPs quenched the FL intensity of N, S-CQDs. Also, the FL intensity reduced with increasing target concentrations. In contrast and in the absence of target, Ag NPs were aggregated and consequently the FL intensity of N, S-CQDs was restored. The LOD of this assay was 13 nM with a linear range between 50 and 200 nM. Eventually, this approach was exploited to detect AD in urine samples of lung cancer patients and showed satisfactory results.

The last study in this section was about the use of CQDs and polymer as a aptasensing platform for the ATP detection [113]. In this regard, Guo et al. prepared positively charged polymeric CQDs (PEI-CQDs) by using malic acid and polyethylenimine (PEI). Briefly, in the presence of ATP Apt with negative charge and sequence of 5′–NH2–(CH2)6-ACCTGGGGGAGTATTGCGGAGGAAGGT-3′, the FL intensity of CQDs was quenched due to the positive-negative interactions between Apt and CQDs. When the ATP was added into the system, the complex of Apt and ATP was formed and released from the CQDs and thus, the FL intensity of CQDs was recovered. In this sensing method, the target can be selectively and sensitively detected the target with a low LOD of 13 nM. This sensing method had some features compared to previous approaches and reduced the background signal and improved the sensitivity of ATP detection.

For the First time, Bard et al. discovered that QDs are able to produce light emissionduring excitation/emission processes, which was called ECL [114]. It is the combination of chemiluminescence (CL) and electrochemistry in which the electron-transfer reactions are happened at an electrode surface to form excited states, and light is generated when the excited fragments decay to the ground state [115–117]. Recently, this strategy has received increasing attention in different fields of science such as food contamination studies, environmental pollutant determination and particularly biological analyses including Apt biosensors [118–121]. Typically, in the ECL aptasensing platforms, the surface of electrode is modified by target Apts and then, they are linked to various M − and C-based QDs to detect different target molecules [122–124]. Thus, in the next section, most of the recent advances in ECL aptasensing platforms based on M − and C-based QDs are discussed in detail.

In fact, after brief search in literature, we realized that only the Cd and Zn hybrid materials with bare and core-shell structures have been exploited for development of ECL aptasensing platforms. So, in this regard and in the first study, a core-shell CdSe–ZnS QD ECL aptasensing platform has been described by Huang and coworkers for ATP detection [125]. They used Apt–target affinity strategy for specific determination of the target. So, firstly, they immobilized the thiol modified ATP Apt on the surface of Au electrode via Au–S bonding. Then, the ATP target was added to the system and the Apt–ATP bioaffinity complexes were formed on the surface of electrode. Next, upon the 5′-biotin-modified complementary DNA introduction into the system, the free immobilized ATP Apts which were not combined with ATP, were hybridized with complementary DNA and the double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) structures were formed. Ultimately, after adding the streptavidin-modified QDs to the system, the dsDNA conjugated QDs were obtained through the biotin–streptavidin bonding. In this aptasensor, the ECL responses were sensitive to the amount of target and QDs. Indeed, the specific binding of ATP with its Apt prevented the formation of dsDNA structures and subsequently reduced the amount of QDs bound to the system, and eventually decreased the ECL intensity. The ECL responses extremely depended on the ATP concentration over the 0.018–90.72 mM range and with maximum amount of ATP concertation (90.72 mM), the response of the system was approximately near zero due to the blocking of all ATP Apts with target. The selectivity of this aptasensing platform was also investigated for other ATP analogues such as UTP, CTP and GTP. The ECL intensities of the system was about 18,000 a.u. for other ATP analogues, while it decreased to 4500 a.u. for ATP which showed a high specific affinity of the system to the ATP detection. The LOD of this ECL aptasensor was around 6 nM.

The similar aptasensing platform has been introduced by Zhou and colleagues by using of core-shell CdSe–ZnS QDs for aptasensing detection of ATP [126]. In this research, the authors employed three-dimensionally ordered macroporous gold film as an electrode for immobilization of ATP Apt. Then, instead of ATP introduction to the system, they firstly added 5′-biotin-modified complementary DNA to form duplex DNA structure and after that, added the ATP and finally the streptavidin-modified QDs. Significantly, by adding the ATP to the system and due to the Apt-ATP affinity, the structure of some duplex DNA structures was denatured and the rest of them were able to hybridized with streptavidin-modified QDs through the biotin–streptavidin bonding (Fig. 6 ). Similar to previously study, this aptasensing platform was sensitive to ATP concentration and this approach could detect the ATP with the LOD of 0.01 nM. Besides, this aptasensing platform had better results in the case of LOD and the ATP could be detected in nanomolar concentration range compared to abovementioned study that was in micromolar concentration range.

The next “Off-On” ECL aptasensing platform was constructed based on CdTe QDs, Au NPs, formation of G-quadruplex structures and reduction reaction of dissolved oxygen [127]. In this project, firstly, the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was modified by QDs functionalized complementary DNA (QDs-DNA1) and then, they were hybridized with ATP Apts. The signal probe was designed by adding of another DNA oligonucleotide (DNA2) and G-quadruplex structures on the Au NPs, that was utilized to link the probe to the electrode through a hybridization reaction of DNA1-aptamer with DNA2. The signal probe with G-quadruplex/hemin structure induced the reduction reaction of dissolved oxygen and simultaneously quenched the ECL response of QDs [128]. Without the ATP, the sandwich hybridization of DNA1-aptamer-DNA2 was formed on the surface of electrode which decreased the ECL emission of QDs and state of the system was “off” in this situation. In the presence of ATP, its affinity to Apt led to disassociation of Apt from electrode surface and decreased the amount of captured signal probe. So the ECL emission of QDs increased and state turned back to “On” (Fig. 7 ). This ECL aptasensing platform could recognize the target down to the nM level in a linear range of 8–2000 nM and with a LOD of 7.6 nM.

The last study in this section was about the use of CdS QDs coated platinum nanostructured networks (QDs-Pt) and graphene/polyaniline in fabrication of an ultrasensitive ECL aptasensing platform based on dual amplification strategy [129]. In this research for the immobilization of first probe, initially, the surface of GCE film was modified by graphene/polyaniline through a new one-step large-scale electrochemical synthesis. Then, the ATP Apt (ssDNA1) was immobilized on the surface of GCE modified graphene/polyaniline. Afterwards, the ssDNA2 labeled with QDs-Pt and ATP were added to the system. Upon adding, the sandwich complex of ssDNA1, ATP and ssDNA2 labeled with QDs-Pt was formed which brought the QDs-Pt in a close proximity of electrode surface. Thus, the ECL response of the system increased obviously. Pt nanostructured networks are excellent carriers in the transduction amplification of recognition events [130]. According to their results, ECL intensity of the system can be affected greatly by the QDs-Pt composite and this well-fabricated QDs-Pt composite exhibited a superior ECL performance compared to bare CdS QDs. Therewith, taking advantage of dual-amplification effects such as high sensitivity and satisfied stability and selectivity, this proposed ECL aptasensing platform was able to detect the target with a LOD of 0.1 pM, in the range from 0.5 pM to 20 nM.

In the case of C-based ECL aptasensing platform, only one published article has been presented based on silica coated GQDs. GQDs, as recently emerging C-based nanomaterials, are graphene sheets honeycomb two-dimensional (2D) lattice with nanoscale size (less than 100 nm) and show high great photoluminescence stability, great thermal conductivity and high mobility of charge carriers [131]. Owing to their unique properties and much lower toxicity compared to M-based QDs especially Cd hybrid materials, recently, they have attracted widespread attention among scientists for biosensing technology and ECL aptasensing platforms [132–134]. Taking into account of these advantages of GQDs, an ECL aptasensing platform has been reported by Lu et al. based on core-shell silica coted GQDs (SiO2@GQDs) for ATP detection [134]. They exploited the SiO2 NPs as a signal carrier and SiO2@GQDs as a signal amplification. The presented GQDs showed bright blue emission under ultraviolet irradiation, and in addition to ECL, they exhibited an excitation-independent photoluminescence feature. On this basis, at first, the surface of Au electrode was modified by thiolated-ATP-Apt (ssDNA1) and Au–S bonding and on the other hand, the core-shell SiO2@GQDs were linked to the complementary ssDNA2. In the presence of the target, a stable complex was formed between the ssDNA1, ATP and ssDNA2 which brought the SiO2@GQDs to a close vicinity of electrode surface, and as a results, the ECL response has been occurred. It is worth to mention that with the increase of ATP concentrations the ECL response has been enhanced proportionally. More importantly, the author claimed that the GQDs were able to show the ECL response in the presence of H2O2 as a coreactant via radial mechanism [135]. The results confirmed that in the absence of H2O2, no obvious ECL signal was observed which indicated the important role of H2O2 as a coreactant. This strategy would develop the new ideas to design the novel ECL aptasensors based on GQDs for various targets determination. The most advantages of such aptasensing platform was the using of GQDs as a low toxicity and eco-friendly materials compared to M-based QDs. What's more, this ECL aptasensing platform showed the great analytical performance for ATP detection, ranging from 5.0 to 5.0 pM with the LOD of 1.5 pM.

These days, a number of various types of PEC sensors have been developed for analytical determination of different biomolecule targets [136–138], because these sensitive detection platforms are able to provide excellent selectivity and specificity due to the completely different forms of energy for excitation and also detection [139]. Employing of PEC sensors have some features such as low cost and simple equipment procedure, low background signal and easy miniaturization. Blending of these unique features of PEC approaches with Apt and QD-based detection methods, creates a new PEC aptasensing platform with high sensitivity and selectivity [140]. Thereby, in the next sections, the PEC aptasensing platforms based on photoactive QDs are discussed and reviewed in detail.

The photoactive materials used in PEC biosensors can be categorized into organic and inorganic types. The organic materials mostly are small molecular dyes and the inorganic photoactive materials normally are QDs or semiconductor nanomaterials. Although, after our careful investigation in literature, only one published article has been found which it was based on Cd hybrid QDs and ruthenium complex as photoactive materials. In this research, Fan et al. reported the PEC aptasensing platform based on CdS QDs, TiO2 and Au NPs, ruthenium complex dye and formation of G-quadruplex structures for ATP detection [141]. Initially, the surface of indium-tin oxide (ITO) electrode has been modified with TiO2 and Au NPs, respectively to generate the TiO2/Au hybrid structure. Then, ATP Apt (pDNA) was linked on the surface of Au NPs by Au–S bonding. Afterwards, glutaraldehyde molecules were used for immobilization of amino-functionalized Mn doped CdS QDs (CdS:Mn) on the terminal of pDNA. Subsequently, ruthenium complex (Ru (bpy)2 (dcbpy)2+) as a photoactive dye was linked on the surface of CdS:Mn QDs via the help of EDC coupling reaction. Eventually, the complementary DNA (cDNA) was conjugated with pDNA to form the duplex DNA structures. Combination of inorganic photoactive materials such as QDs with another inorganic photoactive dye (ruthenium complex) produces the photoactive nanocomposite which effectively amplifies the photocurrent responses of the electrode [142,143]. Briefly, in the presence of ATP, due to the bioaffinity between Apt and ATP, the G-quadruplex structures were formed on the surface of electrode and it made the photoactive nanocomposite very close to the TiO2/Au electrode surface and consequently the photocurrent intensity increased (Fig. 8 ). This aptasensing platform showed a low LOD of 0.18 pM for ATP determination. Furthermore, owing to the great PEC performances, the proposed well-fabricated sensing platform can be extended to recognize other important biological molecules.

CDs are famed due to their unique electric and optical properties and recently they are considered as a promising alternative to other nanocarbon materials such as graphene, carbon nanotubes and fullerenes and also toxic semiconductor QDs [144,145]. Interestingly, CDs have an intrinsic band gap and exhibit outstanding PEC activity [146]. In the case of C-based QD materials, Gao and colleagues introduced an ultrasensitive PEC aptasensing platform based on CDs sensitized-molybdenum sulfide (MoSx/CDs) and dual amplification strategy for AD detection [147]. At first, the surface of ITO electrode was modified by MoSx and CDs materials, respectively to form the visible-light-activated MoSx/CDs heterostructure with high electrical contact to the ITO electrode. Then, the complementary DNA (NH2-DNA) and AD Apt were immobilized on the surface of MoSx/CDs heterostructure by covalent coupling reaction and finally the methylene blue (MB) dye was linked to the terminal moieties of the Apt via the guanine base to construct a dual amplified PEC aptasensing platform. By adding the AD to the system, the MB-linked Apt was released from the surface of electrode and photocurrent response was reduced on the basis of interruption of dual amplification strategy (Fig. 9 ). This proposed aptasensing platform with CDs and MB as the photosensitizer and photoactive probe respectively, was able to detect the AD with a LOD of 3.3 pM.

In addition, for better understanding and comparison the aforementioned optical and electrochemical aptasensing platforms; methods, strategies and LODs of all reviewed aptasensors have been summarized in Table 1 . As shown in this Table, the strategies that have been used for FL aptasensing platforms are including CRET, FERT, FL quenching and FL polarization assay (entries 1–14), while, formation of G-quadruplex structures, Apt–target bioaffinities and dual and signal amplification approaches are the strategies that have been employed in ECL and PEC aptasensing platforms (entries 15–21). More importantly, this Table showed that, mainly, FL C-based QD aptasensing platforms had the lower LODs (pM and nM, entries 8–14) compared to FL M-based QDs (μM and nM, entries 1–7). Moreover, and in general, ECL and PEC aptasensing platforms are much more precise compared to FL aptasensing platforms in the case of LOD and they were in nanomolar and picomolar ranges (entries 15–21).

In this review article, the recent achievements and advances of optical and electrochemical aptasensing platforms based on QDs have been comprehensively summarized for the selective and sensitive ATP and AD determination. The ultimate purpose in design and development of sensors is to achieve the fabrication of a tool that can be bought and sold in the market, and it is one of the most important challenges and perspectives for ATP aptasensing platforms. Most of the researches presented in this review article exhibited the high sensitivity and selectivity, but in real complex samples, there are some specific problems that have to be considered for industrial applications and there is still a long distance for construction of affordable and commercial aptasensor. Thus, with introducing and developing the electrochemical and optical approaches based on new advanced functional materials like QDs and Apts, commercially and precise aptasensing platforms can be designed for sensitive determination of ATP from complex biological samples and this can be an important solution to recognize the ATP for medical and pharmaceutical applications.

On this basis and in the case of detection methods, the optical and electrochemical aptasensing platforms have been investigated and compared according to different methods including FL, ECL and PEC. Typically, we realized that the FL aptasensing platforms operated based on CRET, FERT, FL quenching and FL polarization assay as well, while the ECL and PEC electrochemical aptasensing platforms worked based on formation of G-quadruplex structures, Apt–target bioaffinities and dual and signal amplification strategies. On the other hand, applications of different binary, ternary and quaternary M-based QDs, various CD, CQD and GQD C-based materials and also diverse quenchers such as noble metals, graphite and graphene carbon materials and different organic dyes have been reviewed in design and construction of aptasensing platforms. As described in this article, recently C-based QD materials have been more used in construction of aptasensing platforms compared to M-based ones, because they have easier and cheaper synthetic procedures and more importantly they showed lower toxicity. Also, on the basis of different methods, mainly, FL C-based QD aptasensing platforms exhibited lower LODs compared to FL M-based QDs and generally, ECL and PEC aptasensing platforms are much more precise compared to FL aptasensors in the case of LOD that is the main recognition factor. Hence, in this review article, we tried to highlight and discuss the advantages and shortcomings of various strategies of QD aptasensing platforms. However, QDs aptasensors can upgrade the function of analytical approaches such as specificity and sensitivity, they still involved to be further investigated to secure assurance of consumers.
